issues.title: Mesh: clustered Docker-on-VM deploy baseline for Hyperpush
issues.body:
## Outcome
Give Hyperpush a truthful, repeatable Docker-first deployment baseline on a generic VM with Mesh clustering that works without repo-local magic.

## Scope
- Generic Docker-on-a-VM deployment path for Mesh clustered apps
- Node identity, discovery, clustering, and bootstrap UX must be stable enough for Hyperpush
- Container/runtime contract should be simple enough to document and automate
- Close the gap between "language supports clustering" and "operator can actually deploy this product"

## Acceptance criteria
- A generic VM + Docker path exists and is documented
- The clustered runtime starts, discovers peers, and stays healthy under the Hyperpush shape
- The deploy path is truthful for both single-node and clustered product deployments
- Any required env/entrypoint/runtime assumptions are explicit and tested

## Roadmap notes
- Domain: Mesh
- Track: Mesh Foundation
- Commitment: Committed
- Critical path: 1

<!-- roadmap-detail-start -->
## Detailed acceptance criteria
- Generic Docker-on-a-VM cluster bootstrap is documented and repeatable.
- Peer discovery, identity, and health verification work without repo-local magic.
- Single-node and clustered product deployments are both truthful.

## Initial sub-issues
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/7 — Mesh: define generic Docker cluster bootstrap contract
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/8 — Mesh: ship reference clustered VM deployment and health verification
<!-- roadmap-detail-end -->

issues.title: Mesh: runtime hardening for Hyperpush production load and clustering
issues.body:
## Outcome
Harden the Mesh runtime so Hyperpush can honestly claim production readiness under real clustered load, failover, and recovery conditions.

## Scope
- Cluster stability under Hyperpush production traffic patterns
- Failover, recovery, and continuity behavior required by the product
- Runtime diagnostics and observability needed to debug real incidents
- Any correctness or performance issues that would make Hyperpush's core promises fake

## Acceptance criteria
- Runtime behavior is stable enough for Hyperpush production use
- Known crashers, correctness bugs, or continuity gaps on the Hyperpush path are closed or explicitly removed from public claims
- Cluster health, recovery, and failure surfaces are inspectable by operators

## Roadmap notes
- Domain: Mesh
- Track: Mesh Foundation
- Commitment: Committed
- Critical path: 1

<!-- roadmap-detail-start -->
## Detailed acceptance criteria
- Failover and recovery semantics are stable enough for Hyperpush production incidents.
- Operators can inspect recovery state and failure causes with useful diagnostics.
- Soak or load evidence exists for clustered runtime behavior on the Hyperpush path.

## Initial sub-issues
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/9 — Mesh: harden failover and recovery semantics for Hyperpush workloads
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/10 — Mesh: expose operator diagnostics and soak verification for clustered runtime behavior
<!-- roadmap-detail-end -->

issues.title: Mesh: deploy and release primitives needed for Hyperpush autonomous remediation
issues.body:
## Outcome
Provide the Mesh-side primitives needed for Hyperpush's autonomous remediation loop to deploy fixes, verify releases, and reason about runtime truth safely.

## Scope
- Release/deploy/runtime surfaces needed by Hyperpush's agent loop
- Post-deploy verification hooks or signals needed to confirm error-rate drops
- Support for truthful autonomous deploy + rollback/reopen behavior where appropriate
- Any missing runtime/build/deploy seams that block the GitHub-to-production loop

## Acceptance criteria
- Hyperpush can depend on Mesh/runtime surfaces for deploy + verify flows without hidden operator work
- Post-release verification is grounded in real runtime signals
- Missing primitives are added or the Hyperpush product claim is narrowed truthfully

## Roadmap notes
- Domain: Mesh
- Track: Deployment
- Commitment: Planned
- Critical path: 1 -> 4

<!-- roadmap-detail-start -->
## Detailed acceptance criteria
- Release, deploy, and runtime verification signals are available to the Hyperpush product.
- Post-release verification can rely on those real signals.
- Rollback or regression behavior is supported or explicitly bounded.

## Initial sub-issues
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/11 — Mesh: expose release and deploy verification signals for autonomous remediation
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/12 — Mesh: add rollback and regression primitives for failed autonomous fixes
<!-- roadmap-detail-end -->

issues.title: Mesh: docs, packages, installers, and public surfaces must stay truthful for Hyperpush launch
issues.body:
## Outcome
Keep the Mesh install, docs, packages, and public surfaces truthful as Hyperpush becomes a real product dependency.

## Scope
- Installer/install docs and package surfaces
- Public docs and landing language that Hyperpush depends on
- Self-hosted and clustered deployment guidance
- Any package/docs/website drift that would make the Hyperpush launch dishonest

## Acceptance criteria
- Public docs/packages/install surfaces match the actual runtime and deploy contract
- Hyperpush operators are not sent down fake or stale Mesh paths
- Product-critical Mesh docs are current enough to support onboarding and self-hosting

## Roadmap notes
- Domain: Mesh
- Track: Mesh Foundation
- Commitment: Committed
- Critical path: 1

<!-- roadmap-detail-start -->
## Detailed acceptance criteria
- Public install, package, and docs surfaces match actual runtime and deploy behavior.
- Self-hosted and clustered operator docs reflect the real product path.
- Mesh surfaces referenced by Hyperpush do not overclaim unsupported behavior.

## Initial sub-issues
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/13 — Mesh: audit installer, package, and docs truth against the Hyperpush launch contract
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/14 — Mesh: refresh self-hosted and clustered operator docs for product deployments
<!-- roadmap-detail-end -->

issues.title: Mesh: define generic Docker cluster bootstrap contract
issues.body:
## Parent epic
- hyperpush-org/mesh-lang#3

## Outcome
Define the exact env, identity, discovery, and startup contract for generic Docker deployments of clustered Mesh apps.

## Acceptance criteria
- Operator-facing bootstrap inputs are explicit
- Cluster formation works without repo-local hacks
- The contract is stable enough for Hyperpush product deployments

Provide a reference generic-VM deployment plus health verification flow for clustered Mesh apps under a Hyperpush-shaped workload.

## Acceptance criteria
- Reference deployment exists
- Health and peer discovery are verifiable
- Single-node and clustered paths are both truthful

issues.body:
## Parent epic
- hyperpush-org/mesh-lang#4

## Outcome
Make failover, recovery, and continuity behavior reliable enough for real Hyperpush production incidents.

## Acceptance criteria
- Owner loss and recovery flows are stable
- Continuity semantics match product expectations
- Known correctness gaps on the Hyperpush path are closed

issues.title: Mesh: expose operator diagnostics and soak verification for clustered runtime behavior
issues.body:
## Parent epic
- hyperpush-org/mesh-lang#4

## Outcome
Give operators enough runtime diagnostics and soak evidence to trust clustered Mesh behavior under Hyperpush load.

## Acceptance criteria
- Operators can inspect runtime failures and recovery
- Soak or load evidence exists for the clustered path
- Diagnostic surfaces are useful, not just present

issues.title: Mesh: expose release and deploy verification signals for autonomous remediation
issues.body:
## Parent epic
- hyperpush-org/mesh-lang#5

## Outcome
Provide the release/deploy/runtime signals Hyperpush needs to verify autonomous fixes after deployment.

## Acceptance criteria
- Release/deploy signals are available to the product
- Post-release verification can rely on real runtime data
- Hidden manual operator steps are eliminated or made explicit

issues.title: Mesh: add rollback and regression primitives for failed autonomous fixes
issues.body:
## Parent epic
- hyperpush-org/mesh-lang#5

## Outcome
Support truthful rollback or regression behavior when an autonomous fix is deployed and does not hold.

## Acceptance criteria
- Failed fixes can be detected cleanly
- Product can trigger or reason about rollback/regression actions
- Runtime limitations are explicit where rollback cannot be owned automatically

issues.title: Mesh: built-in runtime observability canvas for nodes, actors, and message flow
issues.body:
## Outcome
Add built-in runtime observability to Mesh so operators can see clustered nodes and actor activity visually: nodes joining and dying, actors spawning and respawning, and data/messages traveling across the system in real time.

## Scope
- Runtime-owned observability surface for node and actor topology
- Structured lifecycle events for node join/leave/failure and actor spawn/exit/restart
- Message/data-flow telemetry suitable for live visualization
- A visual canvas/operator surface that can render nodes, actors per node, traffic between them, and failure/recovery behavior
- A truth-preserving surface that both Hyperpush and self-hosted Mesh operators can consume

## Acceptance criteria
- Operators can see nodes and actor placement visually on a canvas
- Node death, recovery, and cluster membership changes are visible in near real time
- Actor spawn, death, and respawn are visible in near real time
- Message or data movement between nodes/actors is represented visually and grounded in real runtime data
- The observability contract is stable enough to support both product and self-hosted operator use cases

## Detailed acceptance criteria
- Runtime telemetry must expose real node and actor state rather than inferred UI-only models.
- Operators must be able to see node membership, actor placement, spawn/death/respawn, and cross-node flow.
- Failure and recovery visuals must be grounded in real runtime events.
- The observability surface must support both Hyperpush and generic Mesh operator use cases.

## Initial sub-issues
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/16 — Mesh observability: emit structured runtime topology and actor lifecycle events
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/17 — Mesh observability: capture message and work movement for live cluster visualization
- [ ] https://github.com/hyperpush-org/mesh-lang/issues/18 — Mesh observability: build operator canvas for nodes, actors, failures, and respawns

issues.title: Mesh observability: emit structured runtime topology and actor lifecycle events
issues.body:
## Parent epic
- hyperpush-org/mesh-lang#15

## Outcome
Expose a structured runtime event stream for node topology, actor lifecycle, and cluster state transitions.

## Acceptance criteria
- Node join/leave/failure events are emitted
- Actor spawn/exit/restart events are emitted
- Event stream is stable enough for operator tooling and visualization consumers

issues.title: Mesh observability: capture message and work movement for live cluster visualization
issues.body:
## Parent epic
- hyperpush-org/mesh-lang#15

## Outcome
Capture data/message/work movement in a way that can power live visualizations without lying about what the runtime actually knows.

## Acceptance criteria
- Cross-node movement is represented from real runtime data
- Flow semantics are clear enough for operator debugging
- The surface avoids fake animation divorced from real events

issues.title: Mesh observability: build operator canvas for nodes, actors, failures, and respawns
issues.body:
## Parent epic
- hyperpush-org/mesh-lang#15

## Outcome
Build the visual operator canvas that renders node topology, actor placement, flow, and failure/recovery behavior.

## Acceptance criteria
- Nodes are visible on a canvas
- Actors per node are visible
- Node death and actor respawn are visibly represented
- The canvas reflects real runtime state rather than a static mock
